commands.review_summary:
The PR preserves SecretRef markers for nested `models.providers.*.request.*` secrets in generated `models.json` and extends secrets audit coverage for stale plaintext request secrets.

Reproducibility: yes. there is a high-confidence source reproduction path: current main documents request SecretRefs but only preserves top-level provider apiKey/header markers in `models.json`. I did not run tests because this review is read-only.
commands.review_followup: The remaining action is contributor/maintainer review: fix the marker-consumption blocker and provide real behavior proof; ClawSweeper should not repair this automatically while external proof is missing.

target.title: [codex] Preserve request SecretRef markers in models.json
target.body:
## Summary

- Preserve SecretRef markers for nested provider `request` secrets when generating prompt-visible `models.json`, including request headers, auth token/value, TLS material, and proxy TLS material.
- Apply source snapshot markers over resolved runtime values so generated agent config does not persist request-scoped plaintext secrets.
- Extend `openclaw secrets audit` to flag stale generated `models.json` request secrets that may already exist on disk.

## Why

#11829 tracks reducing agent-visible API key exposure. Current main already protects top-level provider `apiKey` and provider `headers`, but `models.providers.*.request.*` is also part of the documented SecretRef credential surface. Without this patch, resolved request header/auth/TLS secrets can still be serialized into `models.json`.

This is a focused Layer 1 hardening PR. It does not claim to close the full roadmap while plaintext config remains supported.

## Real behavior proof

Before the implementation, the new regression test failed because the generated provider kept the resolved runtime header:

```text
Expected: "secretref-env:OPENAI_REQUEST_AUTH_HEADER"
Received: "Bearer runtime-request-header"
```

After the patch, the planner writes non-secret markers for nested request fields and preserves non-secret request metadata such as TLS `serverName`.

The audit regression also failed before the audit change because `providers.openai.request.headers.Authorization` was not reported from generated `models.json`; after the patch it reports request header/auth/TLS plaintext residue.

## Validation

```bash
corepack pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents.config.ts src/agents/models-config.runtime-source-snapshot.test.ts
corepack pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.secrets.config.ts src/secrets/audit.test.ts
corepack pnpm exec oxlint src/agents/models-config.providers.secret-helpers.ts src/agents/models-config.providers.normalize.ts src/agents/models-config.providers.source-managed.ts src/agents/models-config.runtime-source-snapshot.test.ts src/secrets/audit.ts src/secrets/audit.test.ts
corepack pnpm run tsgo:core
corepack pnpm run tsgo:test:src
git diff --check
corepack pnpm run check:changed
```

Results: targeted agents test passed 6/6, targeted secrets audit test passed 25/25, oxlint passed with 0 warnings/errors, both typechecks passed, `git diff --check` passed, and `check:changed` passed after rebasing to the latest main available locally.

I also ran `corepack pnpm run test:changed`. It failed in unrelated agents tests:

- `src/agents/cli-runner.reliability.test.ts`
- `src/agents/subagent-registry.announce-loop-guard.test.ts`
- one timeout in `src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.test.ts`

I checked a clean `origin/main` worktree and the first two tests fail there with the same assertions. The timeout test passed when rerun as a single file. None of these touch the changed models/secrets paths.

Refs #11829
target.author: zr9959
